quote: What got you interested in looking at solar?
stewart_read: Pike asked the right question but accepted a one-line answer ('We would like to install the solar') without any depth probe. The codex is explicit: this is the emotional anchor, and top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Pike moved on in under 10 seconds. The bill was later captured as data ($80/month, 505 kWh) but was never flipped into emotional alignment against the utility. Reactive read holds: this was a checkbox moment, not an anchor moment.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Pike was reading Mark as low-engagement and chose to move efficiently rather than force depth on a passive customer. Rejected — the codex does not support skipping the anchor on passive customers; it supports adapting the approach, not abandoning the beat.
coaching_implication: Show Pike the codex's 'capture + flip' pattern versus his 'capture + move on' pattern. The $80/month bill and 505 kWh number were in hand — the manager should ask Pike what he could have said to make Mark feel something about that number, not just record it.

quote: Just just in in the balance and me and my brothers.
stewart_read: Mark discloses that brothers are co-owners on the title. This is a co-decision-maker signal that requires the same protocol response as a spouse disclosure: include the co-owners in the appointment. Pike acknowledged it ('K. Perfect.') and moved on without asking whether the brothers needed to be present. The appointment was booked with only Mark confirmed. Reactive read holds: Pike treated this as a data point rather than a protocol trigger.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Pike may have assessed Mark as the primary decision-maker and the brothers as passive co-owners. Rejected — the codex is explicit that anyone on the title is a co-decision-maker who must be included. There is no evidence Pike made a deliberate assessment; the response was a reflexive acknowledgment.
coaching_implication: Walk Pike through protocols.adult_child_decision_maker. The question he needed to ask was whether the brothers needed to be on the appointment. If they do and they're not, this sit is structurally vulnerable at the close. Manager should flag this for the closer in the handoff.

quote: It should say this is lying but not real.
stewart_read: This line is likely Mark reading a spam or fraud warning that appeared on the incoming Ion text. If genuine, it is a scam_anxiety_signal — Mark's phone flagged the text as suspicious, and Mark read it aloud. Pike's response was silence followed by a confirmation question about whether the text came through. The trust signal was not addressed at all. Note: transcript quality is uncertain here — this line may be a Deepgram artifact. Flag for audio review before coaching on it. If the utterance is genuine, the codex's scam_anxiety_signal directive applies: the rep should have acknowledged the warning and reframed the appointment as customer-initiated.
alternative_hypothesis: Transcript artifact: the line may not be a real utterance. If it is an artifact, no coaching action is needed on this specific moment. The manager should pull the audio before using this in a 1-on-1.
coaching_implication: Pull the audio at 05:30 before coaching. If Mark genuinely read a spam warning aloud, Pike's silence is a missed trust-repair moment. The reframe available was simple: acknowledge that spam filters sometimes flag legitimate business texts, and tie back to the Tesla/Ion relationship for legitimacy. If it is a transcript artifact, discard.

quote: Let me just try shooting you in from my personal number then because I don't know why it wasn't not popping up.
stewart_read: When the system text failed, Pike's only fallback was his personal phone number. This is the anti-pattern the codex explicitly flags: personal number exposure creates privacy and scale risk for the floor. Notably, Pike had already successfully extracted a verbal readback of monthly kWh figures (04:07–04:47) before pivoting to the photo request — meaning he abandoned a working path (verbal readback) and then fell back to personal number exposure when the photo path failed. The reactive read holds on both counts: the pivot to photo was unnecessary given the verbal data already in hand, and the personal number fallback is a known anti-pattern.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Pike wanted the full bar-graph image for accuracy rather than relying on Mark's verbal estimates. Plausible, but the codex's bill_collection.alternative_paths.elderly_low_tech_protocol is clear — pivot to verbal readback within 2 minutes of friction, not after exhausting other paths. The verbal data was already being collected successfully.
coaching_implication: Two distinct coaching points here. First: Pike had a working verbal readback in progress and abandoned it — show him the transcript from 04:07 to 04:47 and ask why he switched paths. Second: personal number exposure is a floor-level risk. Manager should reinforce the anti-pattern flag from the codex and, once Stewart's automated bill capture ships, migrate Pike to that flow.

quote: We are good to go on getting this design built out for you. The last thing I need to know now is just a good time for my local specialist just to drop by to go over that design as well as answering any questions or concerns you may have just as a free look.
stewart_read: Pike's booking pivot is clean in structure — he moves from bill collection to appointment scheduling without hesitation. However, 'drop by' implies an in-person visit, which conflicts with the codex's phone_appointment_specified requirement. This may be a verbal shorthand rather than a literal in-person booking, but it is ambiguous enough to create a no-show risk if Mark expects someone at his door rather than a phone call.
alternative_hypothesis: Pike may use 'drop by' as idiomatic language for a phone appointment on this floor. If so, the ambiguity is a phrasing gap, not a structural error. The manager should clarify whether in-person or phone appointments are the norm for this market.
coaching_implication: Ask Pike whether this appointment is phone or in-person. If phone, coach him to say 'phone call' explicitly — 'drop by' creates ambiguity that raises no-show risk. If in-person, flag for the closer so they know to show up at the address.

quote: We'll shoot you a reminder text a little bit before just to double check that time still works.
stewart_read: Pike correctly promised a reminder text, which satisfies button_up.text_followup_promise. However, three other required button-up elements are absent: (1) one_hour_block_stated — no mention of blocking an hour; (2) no_driving_at_computer — no instruction to be at a computer and not driving; (3) is_that_fair_handshake — no verbal commitment from Mark. The codex is explicit that skipping the no-driving/computer anchor raises no-show rate, and the is-that-fair handshake is predictive of show rate. The booking is structurally thin.
alternative_hypothesis: Pike may have assessed Mark as a low-friction customer who didn't need the full button-up sequence. Rejected — the codex treats these as required elements regardless of customer warmth, precisely because their absence predicts no-shows.
coaching_implication: Walk Pike through the three missing button-up elements with the transcript open. The reminder text promise is correct — do not flag that. Focus on the missing computer/no-driving anchor and the is-that-fair handshake. These are the two highest-leverage no-show prevention moves he left out.

quote: Do you know how much is the average, like, you guys charge for, beer, kilo?
stewart_read: Mark's post-booking pricing question is an enthusiasm signal: he is already thinking about cost-per-unit, which means he is mentally modeling ownership, not just curiosity. This is a qualified-buyer signal that the rep should have anchored on. Pike's response ('I'm not entirely too sure. That's exactly what we're gonna go over with you in the meeting') is a partial match to the canonical redirect_to_design strategy — he correctly deferred to the specialist — but he opened with a knowledge-gap admission ('I'm not entirely too sure') rather than a curiosity-creation frame. The canonical strategy calls for making the customer curious to see their specific math, not just telling them the meeting will cover it.
alternative_hypothesis: Pike's response is functionally adequate — he did not fabricate a number and did redirect to the meeting. The strategic read would be that he held scope discipline correctly. Partially valid, but the codex's rebuttals.price_estimate_before_design calls for curiosity framing, not just deflection. The admission of uncertainty ('I'm not entirely too sure') undercuts the specialist's authority before the meeting even happens.
coaching_implication: Show Pike the difference between a knowledge-gap deflection and a curiosity-creation redirect. The codex's canonical response for price_estimate_before_design is to make the customer feel that the meeting will give them real numbers specific to their home — not that the rep doesn't know. The manager should ask Pike: what would have made Mark more excited about the meeting after that question, not less?

quote: Oh, so we have to buy them or it's for free now?
stewart_read: Mark's cost question is the clearest flip opportunity in the call. Pike's response — explaining the bill-swap mechanism and no out-of-pocket upfront — is procedurally correct but misses the inversion move entirely. The flip available here: Mark's $80/month bill is the actual cost he is already paying. The bill-swap frame should have made that number feel like the problem, not the solar program. Pike explained the program instead of making the bill feel expensive. Reactive read holds: this is the universal coaching gap the codex identifies across the corpus — bill captured, flip not deployed.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Pike may have judged that Mark needed the basic mechanics explained before any emotional framing would land. Plausible for a confused customer, but the codex's objection_inversion is designed precisely for this moment — the confusion is the opening, not a reason to defer the flip.
coaching_implication: This is the bill-flip coaching moment for this call. The manager should ask Pike: after Mark said 'so we have to buy them or it's for free now,' what was the number Pike had in hand? ($80/month.) What could he have done with that number to make Mark feel something about his current bill before explaining the program? The codex's coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion section has the framework — the manager should walk Pike through it using this exact exchange.
